insight: Frost Brooder is not generally overpowered, but is very strong against the specific rush strategies that beginners learn first. It is notably weak against Gauss Cannons, according to Elyot (developer).
author: awaclus (quoting elyot)
context: Discussion about whether Chill/Frost Brooder is OP at various time controls, with awaclus quoting Elyot's developer commentary on the unit.

insight: When defending against Chill pressure, players who simply follow the breach warning will tend to overdefend and/or get exploited. Calculating the correct defense manually is harder at faster time controls.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion about Chill being OP at all time controls, with awaclus clarifying that the difficulty is on the defending side, not the attacking side.

insight: Buying an Animus specifically to rush into Husks is comparable in cost to buying 2 Engineers into an Animus, but costs 2 extra gold paid earlier. This can be justified when Husks provide immediate value or when Husk synergies (e.g., Huskophore) are in the set.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about the cost-efficiency of opening Animus directly into Husks versus a normal Engineer-into-Animus line.

insight: Purchasing units with the buy action grants them golden armor, but units constructed by other units (e.g., Pixies constructed by Oxide Mixer) do not receive golden armor, unlike Pixies bought directly or constructed by Grenade Mech as part of a buy action.
author: f300xen
context: Question about why Pixies from Grenade Mech get golden armor but Pixies from Oxide Mixer do not.

insight: With a Cauterizer active, Engineers effectively become prompt: since the Cauterizer consumes energy each turn, any Engineer that would normally sit idle instead has its energy absorbed, making it functionally equivalent to a prompt unit for purposes of absorbing or defending on the turn it is purchased.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion about energy sinks making Engineers prompt, confirmed by velizar_ with specific reference to Cauterizer's buildtime-1 engineers.

insight: Vigilant units are not universally prompt: Centurion is vigilant and prompt, but Urban Sentry is vigilant and not prompt. However, if you do not intend to defend with a vigilant unit, designating it for sacrifice and buying a replacement is equivalent to buying a prompt version of that unit.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion clarifying the distinction between vigilant and prompt mechanics, with bky correcting awaclus's broad claim that vigilant units are always prompt.

insight: Electrovore/Engineer pairs cost 6R total, but the bulk of that value (~5R) comes from the attack the Electrovore provides. The actual cost of making an Engineer prompt via Electrovore is only 0-1 gold depending on circumstance, after accounting for the attack value, defensive sacrifice value, and absorb options.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion about the cost of making Engineers prompt using energy sinks, with bky providing a detailed breakdown of the Electrovore/Engineer pair value.

insight: Thorium Dynamo can create player-favored sets when one player naturally gets two Dynamos while the other only gets one, amplifying the economic gap beyond the usual two-drone first-player advantage.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion about sources of player advantage in specific sets, with spiritfryer identifying Thorium Dynamo as a unit that can widen econ gaps based on turn order.

insight: In Savior sets, the player who activates Savior first (clicking plasmas) gains a large attack spike that is very hard to defend. As a rule of thumb, if you are unsure whether to click plasmas in a Savior set, you should be clicking them; slow Savior sets are very rare.
author: p0lari
context: High-level players discussing Savior strategy, with p0lari, silentslayers, and jamberine agreeing that clicking plasmas aggressively is almost always correct.

insight: In Savior mirror games, P2 getting Savior first and initiating plasma clicks in a plasma-click-favored set is effectively an auto-win, similar to being first with Wincer or Lucina in their respective mirrors.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion of Savior mirror dynamics at high level, with silentslayers stating the initiative advantage is decisive in plasma-click-heavy Savior sets.

insight: In Savior sets, P1 turn-3 Savior is generally considered inferior to P1 turn-4 Savior, but P2's turn-3 Savior line is strong enough that it may pressure P1 into the suboptimal turn-3 line. Economic analysis shows P2 turn-3 yields more spare gold by turn 9 (93 vs 67) than P1 turn-3.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer sharing economic analysis from Msven's Savior unit analysis video, showing P2 turn-3 line outperforms P1 turn-3 line economically.

insight: Plasmafier combined with Thunderhead (Thunderdeemer) creates a heavily player-favored set where, as P2, you cannot viably build Thunderhead because the opponent can simply buy a second Redeemer to counter it, and the set lacks soak or drone options to defend with higher econ.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer discussing a set they lost as P2, with p0lari and jamberine analyzing the Thunderdeemer+Plasma line's player-favoritism.

insight: In Lancetooth sets, the unit's power mainly comes from denying the opponent's very first absorb and providing efficient blue spending on turns where blue is normally hard to use well. Typically you only buy two Lancetooths unless the set is very defensive and high-economy.
author: p0lari
context: Discussion of Lancetooth's strategic role and why spiritfryer finds it frustrating, with p0lari giving a nuanced assessment of its actual power source.

insight: The 'get all 4 Dynamos ASAP' opening (e.g., DD/11/D/DCC opening variant) is economically worse than standard lines—trading a green resource and green income for being an engineer and a gold ahead—without providing enough defensive benefit to justify the cost unless you need to be breach-proof immediately, in which case buying fewer Dynamos and more Gauss Cannons is better.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy analyzing a specific Thorium Dynamo rush opening, comparing it mathematically to standard lines and finding it economically inferior.
